– Q.35 Carry ONE mark Each
Q.11The area of the region bounded by the parabola $x = - y ^ { 2 }$ and the line $y = x + 2$ equals
(A) $\frac { 3 } { 2 }$
(B) $\frac { 7 } { 2 }$
(C) $\frac { 9 } { 2 }$
(D)9
Q.12Let A be a $3 \times 3$ real matrix having eigenvalues $1 , 0 ,$ and -1.If $B = A ^ { 2 } + 2 A + I _ { 3 }$ , where $I _ { 3 }$ iis the $3 \times 3$ identity matrix, then which oneof the following statements is true?
(A) $B ^ { 3 } - 5 B ^ { 2 } + 4 B = 0$
(B) $B ^ { 3 } - 5 B ^ { 2 } - 4 B = 0$
(C) $B ^ { 3 } + 5 B ^ { 2 } - 4 B = 0$
(D) $B ^ { 3 } + 5 B ^ { 2 } + 4 B = 0$
Q.13Consider the following statements.(I) Let A and B be two $n \times n$ real matrices. If B is invertible, then $r a n k ( B A ) = r a n k ( A )$ (II) Let A be an $n \times n$ real matrix. If $A ^ { 2 } x = b$ has a solution for every $\ b { b } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n }$ , then $A x = b$ also has a solution for every $\pmb { b } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n }$ Which of the above statements is/are true?
(A)Only (I)
(B)Only (II)
(C)Both (I) and (II)
(D)Neither (I) nor (II)
Q.14Consider the probability space $( \Omega , \mathcal { G } , P )$ , where $\Omega = [ 0 , 2 ]$ and $\mathcal { G } = \{ \phi , \Omega , [ 0 , 1 ] , ( 1 , 2 ] \}$ . Let X and Y be two functions on Ω defined as $X ( \omega ) = \left\{ { \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { \ i f \ \omega \in [ 0 , 1 ] } \\ { 2 } & { \ i f \ \omega \in ( 1 , 2 ] } \end{array} } \right.$ and $Y ( \omega ) = \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { 2 } & { \mathrm { ~ } i f \omega \in [ 0 , 1 . 5 ] } \\ { 3 } & { \mathrm { ~ } i f \omega \in ( 1 . 5 , 2 ] . } \end{array} \right.$ Then which one of the following statements is true?
(A) $X$ is a random variable with respect to $\mathcal { G } ,$ but $Y$ is not a random variable withrespect to $\mathcal { G }$
(B)Y is a random variable with respect to $\mathcal { G } ,$ but X is not a random variable withrespect to $\mathcal { G }$
(C)Neither X nor Y is a random variable with respect to $\mathcal { G }$
(D)Both X and Y are random variables with respect to $\mathcal { G }$
Q.15Let $\Phi ( \cdot )$ denote the cumulative distribution function of a standard normalrandom variable. If the random variable X has the cumulative distributionfunction $F ( x ) = { \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \Phi ( x ) } & { i f x < - 1 } \\ { \Phi ( x + 1 ) } & { i f x \geq - 1 , } \end{array} \right. }$ then which one of the following statements is true?
(A) $\begin{array} { r } { P ( X \leq - 1 ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \end{array}$
(B) $\begin{array} { r } { P ( X = - 1 ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \end{array}$
(C) $\begin{array} { r } { P ( X < - 1 ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \end{array}$
(D) $\begin{array} { r } { P ( X \leq 0 ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \end{array}$
Q.16Let X be a random variable with probability density function $f ( x ) = { \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \alpha \lambda x ^ { \alpha - 1 } e ^ { - \lambda x ^ { \alpha } } \quad } & { i f x > 0 } \\ { 0 \quad } & { o t h e r w i s e , } \end{array} \right. }$ where $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0$ . If the median of X is 1 and the third quantile is 2,then $( \alpha , \lambda )$ equals
(A) $( 1 , \log _ { e } 2 )$
(B)(1,1)
(C) $( 2 , \log _ { e } 2 )$
(D) $( 1 , \log _ { e } 3 )$
Q.17Let X be a random variable having Poisson distribution with mean $\lambda > 0 .$ Then $\textstyle E \left( { \frac { 1 } { X + 1 } } | X > 0 \right)$ equals
(A) $\frac { 1 - e ^ { - \lambda } - \lambda e ^ { - \lambda } } { \lambda ( 1 - e ^ { - \lambda } ) }$
(B) $\frac { 1 - e ^ { - \lambda } } { \lambda }$
(C) $\frac { 1 - e ^ { - \lambda } - \lambda e ^ { - \lambda } } { \lambda }$
(D) $\frac { 1 - e ^ { - \lambda } } { \lambda + 1 }$
Q.18Suppose that X has the probability density function $f ( x ) = { \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { { \displaystyle \lambda ^ { \alpha } } } & { \quad i f \ x > 0 } \\ { 0 } & { \quad o t h e r w i s e , } \end{array} \right. }$ where $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0$ . Which one of the following statements is NOT true?
(A) $E ( X )$ exists for all $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0$
(B)Variance of X exists for all $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0$
(C) $E \left( { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { X } } } \right)$ exists for all $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0$
(D) $E ( \log _ { e } ( 1 + X ) )$ exists for all $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0$
Q.19Let (X, Y) have joint probability density function $f ( x , y ) = \Big \{ \begin{array} { l l } { 8 x y \quad } & { i f 0 < x < y < 1 } \\ { 0 } & { o t h e r w i s e . } \end{array}$ If $E ( X \mid Y = y _ { 0 } ) = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }$ , then $y _ { 0 }$ equals
(A) $\frac 3 4$
(B) $\frac 1 2$
(C) $\frac 1 3$
(D) $\frac { 2 } { 3 }$
Q.20Suppose that there are 5 boxes, each containing 3 blue pens, 1 red pen and 2black pens. One pen is drawn at random from each of these 5 boxes. If the randomvariable $X _ { 1 }$ denotes the total number of blue pens drawn and the random variable $X _ { 2 }$ denotes the total number of red pens drawn, then $P ( X _ { 1 } = 2 , X _ { 2 } = 1 )$ equals
(A) $\frac { 5 } { 3 6 }$
(B) $\frac { 5 } { 1 8 }$
(C) $\frac { 5 } { 1 2 }$
(D) $\frac { 5 } { 9 }$
Q.21Let $\{ X _ { n } \} _ { n \geq 1 }$ and $\{ Y _ { n } \} _ { n \geq 1 }$ be two sequences of random variables and X and Ybe two random variables, all of them defined on the same probability space.Which one of the following statements is true?
(A) $\mathrm { ~ I ~ f ~ } \left\{ X _ { n } \right\} _ { n \geq 1 }$ converges in distribution to a real constant c, then $\{ X _ { n } \} _ { n \geq 1 }$ converges in probability to c
(B)If $\{ X _ { n } \} _ { n \geq 1 }$ converges in probability to X, then $\{ X _ { n } \} _ { n \geq 1 }$ converges in $3 ^ { \mathrm { r d } }$ meanto X
(C)If $\{ X _ { n } \} _ { n \geq 1 }$ converges in distribution to X and $\{ Y _ { n } \} _ { n \geq 1 }$ converges indistribution to Y, then $\{ X _ { n } + Y _ { n } \} _ { n \geq 1 }$ converges in distribution to $X + Y$
(D)If $\{ E ( X _ { n } ) \} _ { n \geq 1 }$ converges to $E ( X )$ , then $\{ X _ { n } \} _ { n \geq 1 }$ converges in $1 ^ { \mathrm { s t } }$ mean to X
Q.22Let X be a random variable with probability density function $f ( x ; \lambda ) = \left\{ { \frac { 1 } { \lambda } } e ^ { - { \frac { x } { \lambda } } } \ \begin{array} { l l } { { i f x > 0 } } \\ { { 0 } } \end{array} \right.$ where $\lambda > 0$ is an unknown parameter. Let $Y _ { 1 } , Y _ { 2 } , \dots , Y _ { n }$ be a random sample ofsize n from a population having the same distribution as $X ^ { 2 }$ If $\begin{array} { r } { \bar { Y } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } Y _ { i } . } \end{array}$ , then which one of the following statements is true?
(A) $\sqrt { \frac { \bar { Y } } { 2 } }$ is a method of moments estimator of λ
(B) $\sqrt { \bar { Y } }$ is a method of moments estimator of λ
(C) ${ \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } { \sqrt { \bar { Y } } }$ is a method of moments estimator of λ
(D) $2 \sqrt { \bar { Y } }$ is a method of moments estimator of λ
Q.23 Let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \dots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample of size $n \left( \geq 2 \right)$ from a population having probability density function
$f ( x ; \theta ) = \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \displaystyle \frac { 2 } { \theta x } ( - \log _ { e } x ) e ^ { - \frac { ( \log _ { e } x ) ^ { 2 } } { \theta } } \quad } & { i f \ : 0 < x < 1 } \\ { \displaystyle 0 \quad } & { o t h e r w i s e , } \end{array} \right.$
where $\theta > 0$ is an unknown parameter. Then which one of the following statements is true?
(A) $\textstyle { \frac { 1 } { n } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } ( \log _ { e } X _ { i } ) ^ { 2 }$ is the maximum likelihood estimator of $\theta$
(B) $\textstyle { \frac { 1 } { n - 1 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } ( \log _ { e } X _ { i } ) ^ { 2 }$ is the maximum likelihood estimator of $\theta$
(C) $\textstyle { \frac { 1 } { n } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \log _ { e } X _ { i }$ is the maximum likelihood estimator of $\theta$
(D) $\textstyle { \frac { 1 } { n - 1 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \log _ { e } X _ { i }$ is the maximum likelihood estimator of $\theta$
Q.24Let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \dots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample of size n from a population havinguniform distribution over the interval $\left( { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } , \theta \right)$ , where $\theta > \frac { 1 } { 3 }$ is an unknownparameter. If $Y = \operatorname* { m a x } \{ X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \dots , X _ { n } \}$ , then which one of the followingstatements is true?
(A) $\textstyle \left( { \frac { n + 1 } { n } } \right) \left( Y - { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \right) + { \frac { 1 } { 3 } }$ is an unbiased estimator of θ
(B) $\textstyle \left( { \frac { n } { n + 1 } } \right) \left( Y - { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \right) + { \frac { 1 } { 3 } }$ is an unbiased estimator of θ
(C) $\textstyle \left( { \frac { n + 1 } { n } } \right) \left( Y + { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \right) - { \frac { 1 } { 3 } }$ is an unbiased estimator of θ
(D)Y is an unbiased estimator of θ
Q.25Suppose that $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots , X _ { n } , Y _ { 1 } , Y _ { 2 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ are independent and identicallydistributed random vectors each having $N _ { p } ( { \pmb \mu } , { \pmb \Sigma } )$ distribution, where Σ is non-singular, $p > 1$ and $n > 1$ . If $\begin{array} { r } { \overline { { \pmb X } } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { \pmb X } _ { i } } \end{array}$ and $\textstyle { \overline { { Y } } } = { \frac { 1 } { n } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } Y _ { i }$ , then whichone of the following statements is true?
(A)There exists $c > 0$ such that $c \left( \overline { { \pmb { X } } } - \pmb { \mu } \right) ^ { T } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } ( \overline { { \pmb { X } } } - \pmb { \mu } )$ has $\chi ^ { 2 } \mathrm { - d i s t r i b u t i o n }$ with p degrees of freedom
(B)There exists $c > 0$ such that $c ( \overline { { \pmb { X } } } - \overline { { \pmb { Y } } } ) ^ { T } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } ( \overline { { \pmb { X } } } - \overline { { \pmb { Y } } } )$ has $\chi ^ { 2 } \mathrm { - d i s t r i b u t i o n }$ with $( p - 1 )$ degrees of freedom
(C)There exists $c > 0$ such that $: \textstyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } ( X _ { i } - { \overline { { X } } } ) ^ { T } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } ( X _ { i } - { \overline { { X } } } )$ has $\chi ^ { 2 } \mathrm { - d i s t r i b u t i o n }$ with p degrees of freedom
(D)There exists $c > 0$ such that $c \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } ( X _ { i } - Y _ { i } - { \overline { { X } } } + { \overline { { Y } } } ) ^ { T } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } ( X _ { i } - Y _ { i } - { \overline { { X } } } + { \overline { { Y } } } )$ has $\chi ^ { 2 } \mathrm { - d i s t r i b u t i o n }$ with p degrees of freedom
Q.26Consider the following regression model $y _ { k } = \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \log _ { e } k + \epsilon _ { k } , ~ k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ,$ where $\epsilon _ { k }$ ’s are independent and identically distributed random variables eachhaving probability density function $\begin{array} { r } { f ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - | x | } , x \in \mathbb { R } } \end{array}$ Then which one ofthe following statements is true?
(A)The maximum likelihood estimator of $\alpha _ { 0 }$ does not exist
(B)The maximum likelihood estimator of $\alpha _ { 1 }$ does not exist
(C)The least squares estimator of $\alpha _ { 0 }$ exists and is unique
(D)The least squares estimator of $\alpha _ { 1 }$ exists, but it is not unique
Q.27Suppose that $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \dots , X _ { n }$ are independent and identically distributed randomvariables each having probability density function $f ( \cdot )$ and median θ. We wantto test $H _ { 0 } \colon \theta = \theta _ { 0 } \qquad \mathrm { a g a i n s t } \qquad H _ { 1 } \colon \theta > \theta _ { 0 } .$ Consider a test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $S > c$ for some c depending on the size ofthe test, where S is the cardinality of the set $\{ i \colon X _ { i } > \theta _ { 0 } , 1 \leq i \leq n \}$ . Thenwhich one of the following statements is true?
(A)Under $H _ { 0 } ,$ the distribution of S depends on $f ( \cdot )$
(B)Under $H _ { 1 } ^ { \phantom { } } .$ , the distribution of S does not depend on $f ( \cdot )$
(C)The power function depends on θ
(D)The power function does not depend on θ
Q.28Suppose that x is an observed sample of size 1 from a population withprobability density function $f ( \cdot )$ . Based on x, consider testing $H _ { 0 } \colon f ( y ) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } e ^ { - \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } ; y \in \mathbb { R }$ $H _ { 1 } \colon f ( y ) = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } e ^ { - | y | } ; y \in \mathbb { R } .$ againstThen which one of the following statements is true?
(A)The most powerful test rejects $H _ { 0 } { \mathrm { ~ i f ~ } } | x | > c$ for some $c > 0$
(B)The most powerful test rejects $H _ { 0 } { \mathrm { ~ i f ~ } } | x | < c$ for some $c > 0$
(C)The most powerful test rejects $H _ { 0 } { \mathrm { ~ i f ~ } } \left| | x | - 1 \right| > c$ for some $c > 0$
(D)The most powerful test rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $\left| \left| x \right| - 1 \right| < c$ for some $c > 0$
Q.29Let $f \colon { \mathbb { R } } ^ { 2 } \to$ R be defined by $f ( x , y ) = x y$ . Then the maximum value (roundedoff to two decimal places) of f on the ellipse $x ^ { 2 } + 2 y ^ { 2 } = 1$ equals

Q.31Two defective bulbs are present in a set of five bulbs. To remove the twodefective bulbs, the bulbs are chosen randomly one by one and tested. If Xdenotes the minimum number of bulbs that must be tested to find out the twodefective bulbs, then $P ( X = 3 )$ (rounded off to two decimal places)equals
Q.32Let $\{ X _ { n } \} _ { n \geq 1 }$ be a sequence of independent and identically distributed randomvariables each having mean 4 and variance 9. If $\begin{array} { r } { Y _ { n } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } \mathrm { ~ f o r ~ } n \ge 1 } \end{array}$ $\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } E \left[ \left( { \frac { Y _ { n } - 4 } { \sqrt { n } } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right]$ then (in integer) equals
Q.33Let $\{ W _ { t } \} _ { t \ge 0 }$ be a standard Brownian motion. Then $E ( W _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } | \ W _ { 2 } = 2 )$ (in integer) equals
Q.34Let $\{ X _ { n } \} _ { n \geq 1 }$ be a Markov chain with state space {1, 2, 3} and transitionprobability matrix ${ \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } } & { { \frac { 1 } { 4 } } } & { { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 4 } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } } } & { { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } } } & { { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } } } \\ { { 0 } } & { { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } } & { { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } } \end{array} \right] } .$ Then $P ( X _ { 2 } = 1 \mid X _ { 1 } = 1 , X _ { 3 } = 2 )$ (rounded off to two decimal places)equals
Q.35 $\pmb { \mu } = \left[ 0 \atop 0 \right]$ Suppose that $( X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , X _ { 3 } )$ has $N _ { 3 } ( { \pmb \mu } , { \pmb \Sigma } )$ distribution withand $\Sigma = { \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 2 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { 5 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] } .$ Given that $\Phi ( - 0 . 5 ) = 0 . 3 0 8 5$ , where $\Phi ( \cdot )$ denotes the cumulativedistribution function of a standard normal random variable, $\begin{array} { r } { P \left( ( X _ { 1 } - 2 X _ { 2 } + 2 X _ { 3 } ) ^ { 2 } < \frac { 7 } { 2 } \right) } \end{array}$ (rounded off to two decimal places)equals
Q.36 Let A be an (I) $n \times n$ real matrix. Consider the following statements. If A is symmetric, then there exists $c \geq 0$ such that $A + c I _ { n }$
symmetric and positive definite, where $I _ { n }$ is the $n \times n$ identity matrix. (II) If A is symmetric and positive definite, then there exists a symmetric and positive definite matrix B such that $A = B ^ { 2 }$
Which of the above statements is/are true?
(A)Only (I)
(B)Only (II)
(C)Both (I) and (II)
(D)Neither (I) nor (II)
Q.37Let X be a random variable with probability density function $f ( x ) = \left\{ { \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } } \qquad i f x \geq 1 \atop 0 t h e r w i s e . \right.$ If $Y = \log _ { e } X ,$ then $P ( Y < 1 \mid Y < 2 )$ equals
(A) $\frac { e } { 1 + e }$
(B) $\frac { e - 1 } { e + 1 }$
(C) $\frac { 1 } { 1 + e }$
(D) $\frac { 1 } { e - 1 }$
Q.38Let $\{ N ( t ) \} _ { t \geq 0 }$ be a Poisson process with rate 1. Consider the followingstatements. $P ( N ( 3 ) = 3 \mid N ( 5 ) = 5 ) = { \binom { 5 } { 3 } } \left( { \textstyle { \frac { 3 } { 5 } } } \right) ^ { 3 } \left( { \textstyle { \frac { 2 } { 5 } } } \right) ^ { 2 } .$ (I)(II) If $S _ { 5 }$ denotes the time of occurrence of the $5 ^ { \mathrm { t h } }$ event for the abovePoisson process, then $E ( S _ { 5 } \mid N ( 5 ) = 3 ) = 7$ Which of the above statements is/are true?
(A)Only (I)
(B)Only (II)
(C)Both (I) and (II)
(D)Neither (I) nor (II)
Q.39Let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \dots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample of size n from a population having probability density function
where $f ( x ; \mu ) = \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { e ^ { - ( x - \mu ) } \quad } & { i f \mu \leq x < \infty } \\ { 0 \quad } & { o t h e r w i s e , } \end{array} \right.$ $\mu \in \mathbb { R }$ is an unknown parameter. If $\widehat { M }$ is the maximum likelihood
estimator of the median of $X _ { 1 }$ , then which one of the following statements is true?
(A) $P \left( \widehat { M } \leq 2 \right) = 1 - e ^ { - n ( 1 - \log _ { e } 2 ) } \mathrm { i f } \mu = 1$
(B) $P \left( { \widehat { M } } \leq 1 \right) = 1 - e ^ { - n \log _ { e ^ { 2 } } } { \mathrm { ~ i f ~ } } \mu = 1$
(C) $P \left( \widehat { M } \leq 3 \right) = 1 - e ^ { - n ( 1 - \log _ { e } 2 ) } \mathrm { i f } \mu = 1$
(D) $P \left( \widehat { M } \leq 4 \right) = 1 - e ^ { - n ( 2 \log _ { e } 2 - 1 ) } \mathrm { i f } \mu = 1$
Q.40Let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots , X _ { 1 0 }$ be a random sample of size 10 from a population having $N ( 0 , \theta ^ { 2 } )$ distribution, where $\theta > 0$ is an unknown parameter.Let $\begin{array} { r } { T = \frac { 1 } { 1 0 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 1 0 } X _ { i } ^ { 2 } } \end{array}$ . If the mean square error of $c T \left( c > 0 \right)$ , as an estimator of $\theta ^ { 2 }$ , is minimized at $c = c _ { 0 }$ , then the value of $c _ { 0 }$ equals
(A)5-6
(B) $\frac { 2 } { 3 }$
(C)3-5
(D) $\frac 1 2$
Q.41Suppose that $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots , X _ { 1 0 }$ are independent and identically distributed randomvectors each having $N _ { 2 } ( { \pmb \mu } , { \pmb \Sigma } )$ distribution, where Σ is non-singular. If $U = \frac { 1 } { 1 + ( \overline { { { X } } } - { \pmb { \mu } } ) ^ { T } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } ( \overline { { { X } } } - { \pmb { \mu } } ) } \ ,$ where $\begin{array} { r } { \overline { { \pmb X } } = \frac { 1 } { 1 0 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 1 0 } { \pmb X } _ { i } } \end{array}$ , then the value of $\begin{array} { r } { \log _ { e } P \left( U \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) } \end{array}$ equals
(A)-5
(B)-10
(C)-2
(D)-1
Q.42Suppose that (X, Y) has joint probability mass function $P ( X = 0 , Y = 0 ) = P ( X = 1 , Y = 1 ) = \theta ,$ $P ( X = 1 , Y = 0 ) = P ( X = 0 , Y = 1 ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } - \theta ,$
where against $\begin{array} { r } { 0 \leq \theta \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \end{array}$ $\begin{array} { r } { H _ { 0 } \colon \theta = \frac { 1 } { 4 } } \end{array}$ is an unknown parameter. Consider testing $\begin{array} { r } { H _ { 1 } ; \theta = \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \end{array}$ , based on a random sample $\{ ( X _ { 1 } , Y _ { 1 } ) , ( X _ { 2 } , Y _ { 2 } ) , \ldots , ( X _ { n } , Y _ { n } ) \}$ from the above probability mass function. Let M be the cardinality of the set
$\left\{ i \colon X _ { i } = Y _ { i } , 1 \leq i \leq n \right\}$ . If m is the observed value of M, then which one of the following statements is true?
(A)The likelihood ratio test rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $m > c$ for some c
(B)The likelihood ratio test rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $m < c$ for some c
(C)The likelihood ratio test rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $c _ { 1 } < m < c _ { 2 }$ for some $c _ { 1 }$ and $c _ { 2 }$
(D)The likelihood ratio test rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $m < c _ { 1 }$ or $m > c _ { 2 }$ for some $c _ { 1 }$ and $c _ { 2 }$
Q.43Let $g ( x ) = f ( x ) + f ( 2 - x )$ for all $x \in [ 0 , 2 ]$ , where $f \colon [ 0 , 2 ] \to \mathbb { R }$ iscontinuous on [0, 2] and twice differentiable on (0, 2). If $g ^ { \prime }$ denotes thederivative of g and $f ^ { \prime \prime }$ denotes the second derivative of $f ,$ then which one ofthe following statements is NOT true?
(A)There exists $c \in ( 0 , 2 )$ such that $g ^ { \prime } ( c ) = 0$
(B)If f" > 0 on (0, 2), then g is strictly decreasing on (0, 1)
(C)If f" < 0 on (0, 2), then g is strictly increasing on (1, 2)
(D) $\mathrm { ~ I f ~ } f ^ { \prime \prime } = 0 \mathrm { ~ o n ~ } ( 0 , 2 )$ , then g is a constant function
Q.44For any subset U of $\mathbb { R } ^ { n }$ , let $L ( { \mathcal { U } } )$ denote the span of U. For any two subsets $\mathcal { T }$ and $\mathcal { S }$ of $\mathbb { R } ^ { n }$ , which one of the following statements is NOT true?
(A)If T is a proper subset of S, then $L ( \mathcal T )$ is a proper subset of L(S)
(B) $L { \big ( } L ( { \mathcal { S } } ) { \big ) } = L ( { \mathcal { S } } )$
(C) $L ( \mathcal { T } \cup \mathcal { S } ) = \{ u + v \colon u \in L ( \mathcal { T } ) , v \in L ( \mathcal { S } ) \}$
(D)If α, β and γ are three vectors in $\mathbb { R } ^ { n }$ such that $\alpha + 2 \beta + 3 \gamma = 0$ then $L ( \{ \alpha , \beta \} ) = L ( \{ \beta , \gamma \} )$
Q.45Let f be a continuous function from [0, 1] to the set of all real numbers. Thenwhich one of the following statements is NOT true?
(A) $\sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } { \frac { f ( x _ { n } ) } { n ^ { 2 } } }$ For any sequence $\{ x _ { n } \} _ { n \geq 1 }$ in [0, 1], is absolutely convergent
